cases.title: Facial Trauma with Bloody Airway
cases.presentation: A 32-year-old male struck in the face during an assault is semi-conscious with blood and broken teeth in his mouth. He has gurgling respirations and is maintaining his own airway poorly. The cervical spine is being held in line.
cases.question: What is the most appropriate immediate airway action?
options: Suction the airway and open it with a modified jaw thrust
Place the patient supine and apply a nonrebreather only
cases.explanation: Gurgling means fluid in the airway—suction first, and open the airway with a modified jaw thrust to protect the cervical spine. Definitive airway management follows once the airway is cleared and the patient is preoxygenated.
cases.clinicalPearl: Suction before you visualize. You cannot intubate or ventilate effectively through a mouth full of blood and debris.

cases.title: Hypoventilating Head-Injured Patient
cases.presentation: An 18-year-old fell from a roof. He is unresponsive with slow, shallow breathing at 6 per minute and snoring respirations. SpO2 is dropping.
cases.question: What is the priority intervention?
options: Open the airway, then assist ventilations with BVM and oxygen at 8–10/min confirming chest rise
Apply a nonrebreather and reassess in 5 minutes
Withhold ventilation until intubation equipment arrives
cases.explanation: Snoring indicates the tongue obstructing the airway—open it (jaw thrust/adjunct). A rate of 6/min with hypoxia is inadequate ventilation, so assist with BVM and oxygen at 8–10/min. The bradycardia and hypertension hint at rising intracranial pressure; avoid routine hyperventilation unless herniation signs appear.
cases.clinicalPearl: Hypoventilation worsens secondary brain injury. Support ventilation to normal rates and oxygenation—do not over-ventilate.

cases.title: Anticipating a Difficult Airway
cases.presentation: A 60-year-old obese patient with a short, thick neck is in a cervical collar after a motorcycle crash. He is breathing but tiring, with SpO2 92% on a nonrebreather. Your protocol allows drug-assisted intubation.
cases.question: Which step best optimizes a safe first intubation attempt?
options: Remove the collar and hyperextend the neck
Apply apneic oxygenation by nasal cannula and prepare external laryngeal manipulation plus a backup BIAD
Attempt intubation repeatedly regardless of saturation
cases.explanation: Obesity, short neck, and in-line immobilization predict a difficult airway (MMAP). Apneic oxygenation prolongs safe apnea, ELM improves the glottic view, and a backup BIAD/plan must be ready. The collar/in-line stabilization is maintained, not removed.
cases.clinicalPearl: Predict difficulty before you act: optimize position, preoxygenate, use apneic oxygenation and ELM, and always have a rescue device ready.
